subheadline: Work with Claude directly in your codebase. Build, debug, and ship from your terminal, IDE, Slack, or the web. Describe what you need, and Claude handles the rest.
value_proposition: An AI coding agent that lives inside your existing dev environment—terminal, IDE, Slack, or web—and autonomously handles tasks from a natural language description all the way to a mergeable pull request.
target_audience: Professional software developers who live in the terminal and IDE and want an AI agent that integrates into their existing workflow rather than replacing it

pricing.model: tiered self-serve with free trial implied via 'Try Claude'
pricing.price_anchoring: Leads with 'included in your Pro plan' to signal low barrier to entry; frames higher tiers as 'great value for everyday use in larger codebases' and 'power users'

features: Works across terminal, IDE (VS Code, JetBrains), Slack, web, and iOS
Manages multiple parallel tasks with visual diffs and server previews
Start a task from your phone and return to a ready-to-merge pull request
Routines: configure once, run on a schedule, API call, or event trigger
Reads local environment context without requiring setup or context file selection
differentiators: 'Built for hackers' — deliberately counter-mainstream positioning vs. GUI-first AI tools
Runs on the local machine with full CLI tool access, not a sandboxed cloud environment
Mobile-to-PR handoff: 'start a task from your phone, come back to a pull request'
Native extensions for VS Code forks (Cursor, Windsurf) and JetBrains
Routines as a first-class feature for scheduled and event-driven automation
trust_signals: Official VS Code and JetBrains marketplace extensions
Available via documented curl/PowerShell install script (developer credibility signal)

design_patterns.layout: Full-width dark hero with animated 'Doodling...' element; tabbed showcase cycling through Desktop/Terminal/IDE/Web/Slack environments; pricing grid; feature narrative sections with alternating text and UI screenshots
design_patterns.color_scheme: Dark background hero with Anthropic's neutral/off-white palette; minimal accent color usage
design_patterns.typography: Clean sans-serif; large hero weight for 'Built for hackers'; monospace used for install commands reinforcing terminal aesthetic
design_patterns.visual_style: Technical and minimal with a deliberate hacker/developer subculture edge
notable_patterns: Tabbed environment switcher in hero to show cross-platform reach
Animated code/drawing element to suggest active intelligence
Three-step narrative block: 'Message from phone → Claude works locally → You get a PR'
Copyable curl/PowerShell install commands inline in hero

emotional_tone: Confident and aspirational with a technical edge; 'hackers' framing signals respect for developer autonomy rather than hand-holding
key_objections_addressed: Works in your existing tools — no new environment to adopt
Included in Pro plan — low cost barrier to try it
Runs on your local machine — addresses privacy/context concerns vs. cloud-only agents
Available for teams and enterprise — not just for individual developers
Works on mobile for asynchronous delegation — addresses the 'I'm not at my desk' use case
missing_or_weak: Zero social proof: no customer logos, testimonials, or user/company count
No video demo or GIF showing the agent actually completing a real task end-to-end
No security or compliance section despite enterprise targeting
No comparison to competitors (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, Devin) even implicitly
No success metrics (e.g., 'reduces time to PR by X%', 'used by N developers')
Pricing section buries the free-entry tier; 'included in Pro' is undersold
Routines feature gets a single callout but no explanation of what it can actually do
standout_elements: 'Built for hackers' headline — rare willingness to narrow the audience signal trust with the target persona
Phone-to-PR narrative is a genuinely compelling and concrete workflow story
Tabbed environment switcher effectively shows breadth without overwhelming
Install command directly in the hero — no friction between intent and action for CLI-comfortable users
Routines positioned as a product differentiator, not just a setting
